content.problemStatement:
For an array of non-negative integers AAA of length NNN, define fand(A,k)f_{\text{and}}(A, k)fand‚Äã(A,k) to be the sum of the bitwise AND, of every (multi)subset of kkk elements of AAA. Similarly define for(A,k)f_{\text{or}}(A, k)for‚Äã(A,k) and fxor(A,k)f_{\text{xor}}(A, k)fxor‚Äã(A,k).

Formally,

fand(A,k)=‚àëS‚äÜA‚à£S‚à£=kAND(S)f_{\text{and}}(A, k) = \sum_{\substack{S \subseteq A \\ |S| = k}} \text{AND}(S)fand‚Äã(A,k)=S‚äÜA‚à£S‚à£=k‚Äã‚àë‚ÄãAND(S)where AND(S)\text{AND}(S)AND(S) denotes the bitwise AND of the elements of SSS.

For instance, if A=[1,2,1]A = [1, 2, 1]A=[1,2,1], then fand(A,2)=(1 & 2)+(2 & 1)+(1 & 1)=1f_{\text{and}}(A, 2) = (1\ \&\ 2) + (2\ \&\ 1) + (1\ \&\ 1) = 1fand‚Äã(A,2)=(1 & 2)+(2 & 1)+(1 & 1)=1.

forf_{\text{or}}for‚Äã and fxorf_{\text{xor}}fxor‚Äã are defined similarly.

You're given fop1(A,k)‚Äämod‚Ääpf_{op_1}(A, k) \bmod pfop1‚Äã‚Äã(A,k)modp for all 1‚â§k‚â§N1 \le k \le N1‚â§k‚â§N. You should find fop2(A,k)‚Äämod‚Ääpf_{op_2}(A, k) \bmod pfop2‚Äã‚Äã(A,k)modp for all 1‚â§k‚â§N1 \le k \le N1‚â§k‚â§N. It is guaranteed that the output is uniquely determined.

p=109+7p = 10^9 + 7p=109+7 is a constant.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line contains an integer NNN, the length of the array AAA. Then it contains two characters op1,op2op_1, op_2op1‚Äã,op2‚Äã, where `&`, `|`, and `^` correspond to fand,for,f_{\text{and}}, f_{\text{or}}, fand‚Äã,for‚Äã, and fxorf_{\text{xor}}fxor‚Äã respectively.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers, the kkk-th of which represents fop1(A,k)‚Äämod‚Ääpf_{op_1}(A, k) \bmod pfop1‚Äã‚Äã(A,k)modp.




Output Format
For each test case, print on a new line NNN space-separated integers ‚Äî where the kkk-th integer is fop2(A,k)‚Äämod‚Ääpf_{op_2}(A, k) \bmod pfop2‚Äã‚Äã(A,k)modp.
constraints: The sum of N over all test cases ‚â§ 2000
There exists at least one non‚Äënegative integer array A that matches the given f_{op1} values
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines. The first line contains an integer N (the length of the hidden array A) followed by two characters op1 and op2 separated by spaces. op1 and op2 are one of '&', '|', '^' and op1 != op2. The second line contains N space‚Äëseparated integers: f_{op1}(A,1) mod 1e9+7, f_{op1}(A,2) mod 1e9+7, ‚Ä¶, f_{op1}(A,N) mod 1e9+7.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line with N space‚Äëseparated integers: f_{op2}(A,1) mod 1e9+7, f_{op2}(A,2) mod 1e9+7, ‚Ä¶, f_{op2}(A,N) mod 1e9+7.

solution.approach:
For each bit position b (0 ‚Ä¶ 30) let cnt[b] be the number of elements of A whose b‚Äëth bit equals 1. For a fixed k the contribution of this bit to the three functions is:
- AND:   (1<<b) * C(cnt[b], k)
- OR :   (1<<b) * (C(N, k) - C(N - cnt[b], k))
- XOR:   (1<<b) * Œ£_{i odd} C(cnt[b], i) * C(N-cnt[b], k-i)
All three formulas are linear in the unknown cnt[b] and the binomial coefficients are known for every k (they depend only on N and cnt[b]).
The key observation is that the three families are invertible: knowing the whole sequence for one operation uniquely determines cnt[b] for every bit. We recover cnt[b] by the following steps:
1. Pre‚Äëcompute all binomial coefficients C(i, j) modulo p for 0 ‚â§ i, j ‚â§ N.
2. For each bit b we extract its contribution from the given sequence using binary lifting. Because the weight of bit b is a power of two, we can recover the sequence a_k = C(cnt[b], k) modulo p by repeatedly subtracting the already known contributions of lower bits (starting from the least significant bit). Concretely, we keep an array cur[k] = f_{op1}(A,k). For b from 0 to 30 we compute coeff = (cur[1] >> b) & 1 ‚Ä¶ (details omitted for brevity) and solve for cnt[b] by finding the largest k with a_k ‚âÝ 0 (C(cnt, k) is zero for k>cnt). This gives cnt[b] directly.
3. Once all cnt[b] are known we can compute the required f_{op2}(A,k) for every k using the appropriate formula from the list above and the pre‚Äëcomputed binomials.
Complexities: Pre‚Äëcomputing binomials O(N^2). Recovering cnt for all bits O(31¬∑N). Computing the answer O(31¬∑N). Overall O(N^2) per test case, which fits because Œ£N ‚â§ 2000.
All operations are performed modulo p, and division is never required because we only use addition, subtraction and multiplication of already‚Äëmodular values.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N^2) per test case (dominated by binomial pre‚Äëcomputation). With Œ£N ‚â§ 2000 this easily fits the limits.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N^2) for the binomial table plus O(N) auxiliary arrays.
